meta.title: Vaerum: Heirs of the Ether
meta.description: A digital card table for friends. Move and flip cards freely; follow the official rulebook yourselves.

ui.deckReset: The deck has been gathered and shuffled.
ui.joinAria: Enter a room code to join
ui.newRoom: A new room has been opened.
ui.waiting: You are the only one here. Share the room link to play with friends.
ui.pasteJoin: Paste code or link to join
ui.invalidCode: No valid room code on the clipboard
ui.connLost: Connection lost. Reconnecting; your moves are saved and will sync.
ui.connRestored: Back online. Your moves are in sync.

diag.title: Connection self-test
diag.subtitle: Checks whether multiplayer sync is set up correctly.
diag.verdictOk: All good. Live multiplayer sync is working.
diag.verdictBad: Multiplayer sync is not working yet. See the note below.
diag.error: The test could not run. Please try again.
diag.hint_config-missing: Set SUPABASE_URL and SUPABASE_ANON_KEY in your hosting environment (Vercel), then redeploy. You can verify them by opening /api/config in your browser; both values must be filled in.
diag.hint_url-bad: The project address must be exactly https://your-project.supabase.co, with no extra path. Copy it from your Supabase dashboard under Project Settings, Data API.
diag.hint_rest-failed: The project could not be reached, or the anon key was rejected. Confirm the URL and the anon (public) key, and make sure the Supabase project is not paused.
diag.hint_realtime-failed: The project is reachable but the Realtime socket did not connect. In your Supabase dashboard, make sure Realtime is enabled, and check that no firewall or proxy is blocking the websocket.

etherResonance.def: When the draw deck runs out, play pauses and the Discard is shuffled into a fresh deck (once per cycle). Each player then sacrifices one card: a Seal or Servant of their choice from their tableau, or, only if their tableau is empty, one card at random from their hand. If both the deck and the Discard are empty, nothing happens.

ascension.def: At the end of your Closing phase, with at least 3 Seals on your tableau, you may declare Ascension (this is irrevocable). Every rival then gets one full turn, the Trial, to destroy, steal or disable your Seals. If you still hold at least 3 Seals when the turn returns to you, before your Focus, you win the game.

servantShield.def: While a player has at least one Servant on their tableau, rivals cannot target that player's Seals at all. Every Servant must be destroyed first, even a targetable one such as Runic Warden, before any Seal can be reached.

hand.def: Your hidden cards. Rivals see the count, not the contents. The hand limit is 7 (excess is discarded at Closing); Seals and Servants do nothing while in your hand.

tableau.def: Your face-up zone for Seals and Servants: at most 4 Seals and 3 Servants (separate limits). Their effects work only here, never from the hand. A card you place is committed: it stays until it is destroyed, sacrificed, discarded to make room, or stolen, and never returns to your hand.

deck.def: The shared face-down central pile you draw from. When it empties, Ether Resonance shuffles the Discard into a fresh deck.

discard.def: The shared face-up pile of spent Spells, cancelled cards, and destroyed Seals and Servants.

focus.def: Phase 1 of your turn: draw 2 cards from the deck (3 or more with Time Rift in play).

action.def: Phase 2 of your turn: spend your 2 HP (up to 5 with Crimson Monolith) on any mix of Create, Study and Cleanse. Unspent HP is lost.

closing.def: Phase 3 of your turn: every active Seal trigger fires, then you discard down to the hand limit of 7. After Closing you may declare Ascension.

create.def: An Action (1 HP): cast 1 Spell from your hand, or place 1 Seal or 1 Servant onto your tableau. If that tableau row is full, you may discard one of your own of the same type to make room, at no extra HP.

study.def: An Action (1 HP): draw 1 extra card from the deck.

cleanse.def: An Action (1 HP): discard 1 card from your hand, then draw 1. This swap keeps your hand the same size.

hp.def: The action points for your turn: 2 by default, up to 5 with Crimson Monolith. Create, Study and Cleanse each cost 1 HP; Interventions cost none. Unspent HP does not carry over.

trial.def: After you declare Ascension, every rival gets one full turn to destroy, steal or disable your Seals before the turn returns to you.

untargetable.def: Immune to enemy actions. Untargetability is layered: each independent protective layer must be removed on its own before the card beneath it can be targeted.

kick.title: Remove player
kick.body: Remove {name} from the table? They will be sent to a new, empty room.
kick.kicked: The host removed you from the table.

shortcutsList.title: Shortcuts
shortcutsList.subtitle: Most actions work on the card under your cursor; a few, like tidying your area, act on your own hand. Empty space does nothing.

items.desc: Move the card under the cursor
Move the whole stack under the cursor
Flip the stack under the cursor (a single card flips alone)
Flip the single card under the cursor
Flip the whole stack under the cursor
Rotate 90° sideways (not a flip); over a pile it turns and squares up the whole stack
Gather the stack under the cursor
Shuffle the stack under the cursor

View the table from your left-hand neighbour's side; press again to return to your own. This changes only your own view, nothing for anyone else — handy for bringing your own area square in front of you.

Tidy your own area. Every card you hold there settles into a neat, deck-like layout: matching cards stacked together, sorted by type and centred, never crossing into a neighbour's space. Only you hear it; everyone sees your cards slide into place.

shortcutsList.mobileNote: On touch there is no mouse or keyboard. Long-press a card to open its action bar: from there you can flip it, turn it sideways, gather or shuffle its stack, read its details, or turn your view of the table. Long-press one of your own cards and you can also tidy your whole area into a clean layout.

leaveConfirm.title: Exit room?
leaveConfirm.body: You will leave this room and a fresh one opens with a new link, with you as its host. Any cards you owned here are released to the other players.

roomFull.title: This room is full
roomFull.body: All four seats are taken. Open your own room and invite friends to play with you there.

joinByCode.title: Join a room
joinByCode.body: Paste a room code or invite link below, then Join. You will leave your current table.
joinByCode.label: Room code or invite link

settings.title: Settings
settings.resetDefaultsHint: Return music, effects and master volume to their recommended levels.

support.title: Support Vaerum
support.intro: Vaerum is built by one independent designer. Your support funds the next steps:
lines: The game and its ideas: new card cycles, sharper balance, and more depth and atmosphere.
This online table: new features, faster and smoother play, and more languages.
A 3D / Steam edition: a fully animated 3D table with rich effects and sound.
A physical card set: premium card stock, embossed sigils, and a proper boxed edition.
support.noUrl: A support page is not set up yet. Please check back later.
support.supportersHint: Add #vaerum to your support message and your name joins this wall within a few days (usually 48-72 hours).
support.thanks: Thank you for keeping the table alive.

entries.title: Tidy your whole area in one press
items: Press D — or, on touch, long-press one of your own cards and tap Tidy your area — to lay your hidden area out as a clean deck: matching cards stacked together, sorted by type and centred, however many you are holding. It never spills into a neighbour's space.
Only you hear it settle; everyone else simply sees your cards slide into place, still hidden behind their backs.
Clearer help text along the way: Shortcuts now lists D next to Gather and Shuffle, and the touch action bar reads in one natural voice instead of singling out a single key.

entries.title: Smoother view turns and tidier info
items: Turning the table with V works from every angle now: you can drag cards into the left and right hand areas after a turn, and you can press V while holding a card so the table spins under it and you place it from the new side.
On touch, the turn-view button now lives in a card's action menu (long-press a card) instead of floating in the corner.
The guide's turn line, like "Turn: Player (you)", is no longer cut off.
In the rulebook, the info bubble for a highlighted term now sits centred just above it, matching the card info panel.

entries.title: Privacy, presence and a more alive table
items: Your hand stays private: an opponent's cards are never shown, not even for a moment while the table is loading.

Players who drop are no longer kicked automatically. Their seat is kept until they leave or the host removes them. If the host drops, hosting passes to someone present and comes back to them when they return.
Clearer connection status, with a brief note when live sync drops or comes back.
You now hear other players' flips and shuffles, not just see them, so the table feels alive. Moves inside a hidden hand stay private and silent.

Polish: a dragged card sits cleanly on its shadow, the first-player list always shows everyone seated, text is easier to read, there are more player names, and the music reshuffles fresh in each room.

entries.title: A smoother, clearer table
items: Player names and the green status light always stay clear above the hand areas, and a card you move past another player's area now stays on top of it.
The table appears fully drawn instead of popping its card art in. If you come back to the tab after a while, you'll see a brief loading only when something needs to reload.
Better fit on phones: the deck and discard piles no longer overlap, and the guide no longer covers the logo or the menu button.
Turning a pile over no longer rotates it upright on its own. It just turns over, the same as a single card. To square a pile up, use Gather.

entries.title: Guide refinements
items: Pressing Start now just begins the guide and leaves your cards exactly as they are. If you want a fresh shuffle first, use Reset deck.
Restart sits with the guide's own buttons. It resets only the guide, never your cards, and asks you to confirm first.

entries.title: A built-in guide for new players
items: There is now an optional Guide that walks the whole table through a game, one step at a time: setup, who goes first, and each player's turn. It only suggests what to do. You are still free to move, flip and shuffle cards however you like.

The host opens the Guide from the menu and it appears for everyone. The host runs the setup steps and starts or restarts the game from inside the panel. During play, the player whose turn it is taps the check to finish each phase.
The panel shows whose turn it is and the current phase in its top bar. You can collapse it to that bar to save space and expand it again at any time.
Reset deck and the Guide controls are now host only, so a game is never reset by accident.

entries.title: A bigger, clearer table
items: Cards are larger and sharper, and they now scale by the same proportion on every screen, from a small phone to a 4K TV, so the table feels substantial everywhere instead of a small island on big monitors.

Your private area is now much wider and more spacious: it reaches into the table corners, so every seat gets a large, identical hand area. A card reads as private the moment it enters your area and public as it leaves, the same for you and everyone watching.
Polish throughout: crisper card edges, premium frosted trays for the private zones, and a clearer shadow under a lifted card or pile.
Fixed: the header menu could not be closed with the keyboard from its own button (Space or Enter reopened it). It now closes as expected.

entries.title: Reset deck works for everyone
items: Reset deck now works for any player at the table, not just the host. Tapping it opens a confirmation, and confirming gathers every card and returns them to a freshly shuffled, face-down deck in the centre (the start-of-game setup) for all players. Before, only the host could do this and for everyone else the button did nothing.

When you gather, shuffle or turn a pile (including the deck), it again squares upright to YOUR own view, the same as the rest of the table. A pile you tidy reads straight to you; the other seats see it at their own angle.

entries.title: One deck, one angle for everyone
items: The shared deck and discard now rest at one steady angle for the whole table. Before, a player on the left or right squaring the deck could leave it lying sideways for everyone else and flip it around each time someone tidied it. Now it sits like a real deck on a table (the side seats just see it edge-on) and it looks the same to all players no matter who shuffles.

entries.title: Shuffling always works
items: Shuffling, flipping or grabbing a pile right after another player handled it now works straight away. A rare hiccup could leave the cards looking locked to the other player for a few seconds, so their shuffle seemed to do nothing; that hold is now released reliably.
The dashed "someone is holding this" outline no longer flickers on top of a card while you are the one picking it up or turning it.

entries.title: Cleaner lifting
items: Lifting a deck no longer casts a heavy shadow from every card at once, which used to bury whatever sat beneath the pile in darkness. The pile now floats with one clean shadow and the table stays readable underneath it.

entries.title: A bigger, richer, quieter table
items: Cards are bigger and clearer, especially on large screens, so the table feels like real cards in hand rather than a small island in the middle. The deck and discard spots grow with them. Nothing about who can see or group a card changed.
Cards look more like real printed cards: the deck back has more depth, a face-up card has a crisp framed edge, and a card you pick up casts a deeper shadow. Still calm and monochrome.

The table surface has a richer, more tactile feel, with a fine grain and a soft depth shadow toward the edges, so the felt looks deliberate and premium instead of flat, while keeping the dark, quiet mood.

Gathering a pile that is already neatly collected now does nothing, instead of re-collecting it and replaying the sound every time. A scattered or fanned pile still gathers as before, and shuffling or turning a pile over stay repeatable.
Dropping a whole pile now lands with a deeper, weightier thud that sounds different from the crisp tap of a single card, so the table sounds the way it looks.

If you turn on your device's 'reduce motion' setting, flipping and shuffling are now fully instant: no card stays briefly hidden or frozen afterwards, and the pile is never momentarily locked for the other players.
A face-down deck that had been turned a full circle now stays put on the deck spot when you resize the window, instead of slowly drifting off it.
Join by code is stricter: it only accepts a real six-character code or an invite link, instead of pulling a wrong code out of a longer pasted word, so you no longer land in an empty, unrelated room.
While you are reading the rules or in any menu, the other players no longer see your pointer frozen on the table; it hides until you come back.

entries.title: Sharper privacy, clearer table, smoother sound
items: Your private area now hides a card the moment any part of it enters, from any side, and shows it again only once it is almost fully out. It looks the same for everyone, on every screen size.
The dark table is easier to read: cards stand out from the felt and the deck and discard labels are clearer, while the calm, dark mood stays.
Music and sound start cleanly on your first tap, with no console warnings after you switch tabs and come back.
If the host's connection drops for a moment, they stay the host while away, instead of it passing to another player.
The supporters wall now lists the most recent supporters first.

entries.title: Smoother table, steadier play
items: Flipping, gathering and shuffling a pile now move like real cards, with no flicker or cards overlapping out of order.
Turning a deck over reads as one clean motion, and mixed face-up/face-down piles tidy themselves as they turn.
Cards and panels no longer slide in from a corner; the card info and the touch action bar appear right where you are.
Joining, leaving, being removed and reconnecting now stay consistent for everyone at the table.
Added a supporters wall and this What's new panel.

feedback.title: Send feedback
feedback.intro: Found a bug, or have an idea or request? Tell us, it helps shape the game.
feedback.none: No feedback channel is configured yet. Please check back later.

legalDoc.title: About & Legal
legalDoc.subtitle: What Vaerum is, and how it treats your data.
legalDoc.intro: Vaerum is a free, browser-based digital card table for playing with friends. This page explains what it is, what data it does and does not handle, and the terms of use.

sections.title: How this table works
body: Vaerum gives you and your friends a shared table. You deal, move, flip, gather, shuffle and rotate the cards together in real time. It never enforces the rules, so you play by the rulebook yourselves, just as you would in person.

Each player has a private area along their own edge of the table. Any card you place in your area is hidden from the others, who see only a blurred card back. Drag a card out of your area to reveal it to everyone, and back in to hide it again. A card switches between hidden and visible as it crosses the edge of your area, and it looks the same to every player on any screen.

The basics: drag a card to move it, or hold Ctrl while dragging to move a whole stack. Scroll over a card to flip it, or right-click (Ctrl-scroll) to flip a whole pile. Press G to gather a loose pile into a tidy stack, M to shuffle it, Shift-scroll to turn it sideways, and D to lay your whole area out in a neat, grouped deck. On a phone, press and hold a card to open its quick actions. You can find the full list under Shortcuts in the menu.

The menu also has two host controls. Open guide shows the rulebook walkthrough to the whole table: it explains setup, who goes first, and each player's turn, and the player whose turn it is taps the check to move the step on. The host runs the setup steps and can Start or Restart the game from inside the panel, which gathers and shuffles the deck for a fresh start. Reset deck simply returns every card to one shuffled pile. The guide only suggests. It never restricts your play.

sections.title: About
body: Vaerum: Heirs of the Ether is a digital tabletop for a 2 to 4 player strategy card game. It gives you and your friends a shared table: deal, move, flip, group and shuffle cards in real time. It does not enforce the rules for you, you follow the rulebook yourselves, exactly as you would around a physical table.

It is built as a small, fast web app and runs entirely in your browser. Live play between devices uses a real-time messaging service to relay each table's moves; there is no game server storing your play.
Vaerum is made by an independent designer. It is offered free to play.

sections.title: Privacy
body: Vaerum is built to need as little of your data as possible.
**No accounts.** You do not sign up or log in. There is no name, email, or password to give.
**No tracking or advertising.** Vaerum runs no analytics, no advertising, and no third-party trackers. Your play is not profiled or sold.

**What stays on your device.** Your volume settings, chosen language, a random display name, and a short-lived room identity are saved in your browser's local storage so the table remembers them between visits. You can clear them any time through your browser settings.

**What travels between players.** While a room is live, the moves on the table (card positions, flips, cursors) are relayed through a real-time messaging service so the other players in your room see them. This data is transient: it is delivered to the players present and is not stored on a server after it is sent.
**The room link.** A room is identified by a short code in its link. Anyone who has the link can join that room, so share it only with the people you want at your table.

sections.title: Terms of use
body: Vaerum is provided for personal, non-commercial enjoyment, as is and without warranty of any kind. It may be unavailable or change at any time.
Please use it respectfully: do not abuse the service, attempt to disrupt other players' games, or use it for anything unlawful.
To the maximum extent permitted by law, the author is not liable for any loss or damage arising from the use of, or inability to use, Vaerum.
Continued use means you accept these terms.

sections.title: Copyright
body: The Vaerum name, the game design and rules, all card names and text, the sigils, and the visual identity are the original work of the author and are protected by copyright.

You are welcome to play Vaerum with friends and to stream or review it with credit. Reproducing, reprinting, redistributing, translating, or adapting the game or its content into any medium, or any commercial use, requires the author's written permission.
© 2026 the Vaerum author. All rights reserved.

seal.name: Seal
seal.description: A permanent structure placed on your tableau. Passive power. The path to Ascension.

spell.name: Spell
spell.description: A one-shot attack played from the hand for 1 HP. Resolved, then discarded.

intervention.name: Intervention
intervention.description: A reactive card. Costs no HP and may be played at any moment, even out of turn.

servant.name: Servant
servant.description: A living shield on your tableau. Opponents must clear your Servants before they can target your Seals.

timeRift.name: Time Rift
timeRift.effect: During your Focus phase you draw 3 cards instead of 2. Each additional copy adds +1 draw (max 4 copies, 6 cards). Becomes active from the turn AFTER it is placed.
timeRift.flavor: A hairline crack in the hour, widening with every breath.

veilOfVoid.name: Veil of Void
veilOfVoid.effect: Your other Seals and your hand cannot be targeted by enemy spells or effects. Veil of Void itself remains targetable. Active immediately when placed. Multiple copies do not stack.
veilOfVoid.flavor: Where it falls, the world forgets to look.

crimsonMonolith.name: Crimson Monolith
crimsonMonolith.effect: During your Action phase you spend 3 HP instead of 2. Each additional copy adds +1 HP, but your total HP can never exceed 5. Becomes active from the turn AFTER it is placed.
crimsonMonolith.flavor: It still stands when all else has fallen.

necromancersEye.name: Necromancer's Eye
necromancersEye.effect: At the start of your Closing phase, take the top card of the Discard into your hand (one more per extra copy). It works the turn it is placed; if the Discard is empty, nothing happens. (It draws before the hand-limit check, so it never costs you a card you wanted; see the rulebook.)
necromancersEye.flavor: The dead keep their accounts in long, patient ledgers.

etherStrike.name: Ether Strike
etherStrike.effect: Spend 1 HP. Destroy 1 card on a rival's tableau. The Servant Shield rule applies: if the rival has any Servant, you must target a Servant first. If the chosen target is untargetable, the spell cannot be cast, no HP is spent.
etherStrike.flavor: A clean cut between the seen and the sealed.

shadowTheft.name: Shadow Theft
shadowTheft.effect: Spend 1 HP. Choose a rival. Their hand is shuffled face-down so no one can see it, then one card is taken from it at random into your hand; neither of you chooses which. If their hand is empty or protected by Veil of Void, this spell cannot be cast and no HP is spent.
shadowTheft.flavor: Every secret has a weight you can lift.

ancientSight.name: Ancient Sight
ancientSight.effect: Spend 1 HP. Draw 3 cards from the deck. It only ever benefits you; no opponent is targeted. If the deck empties mid-draw, Ether Resonance resolves first, then the remaining draws come from the new deck.
ancientSight.flavor: Open the eye that never blinks.

mindParasite.name: Mind Parasite
mindParasite.effect: Spend 1 HP. Move one Servant from a rival's tableau to yours; it keeps its ongoing effects, now under your control, and counts toward your 3-Servant limit. You need a free Servant slot: with 3 already, or if the target has no Servant, it cannot be cast and no HP is spent.
mindParasite.flavor: Loyalty is a door, and you have the key.

twistOfFate.name: Twist of Fate
twistOfFate.effect: Spend 1 HP. Exchange every card in your hand with every card in a chosen rival's hand. Tableau cards are unaffected. Hand-limit excess is resolved by each player at their next Closing phase. Cannot be cast if the target is protected by Veil of Void.
twistOfFate.flavor: Fate's wheel turns, and asks no one.

silence.name: Silence!
silence.effect: Costs no HP. Played at any moment, on anyone's turn, to instantly cancel a freshly cast Spell or Intervention. The cancelled card and Silence! both go to the Discard. Silence! can be cancelled by another Silence!, the topmost Silence! cancels the one below.

karmicReflection.name: Karmic Reflection
karmicReflection.effect: Costs no HP. Play it only to cancel an attack aimed at you or at a card you control in play (your Seals and Servants on your tableau). It does not shield your hand or your deck, and it cannot protect another player. The cancelled attack rebounds onto the attacker and resolves against them in full. On the rebound, the attacker's own Servant Shield applies: if they have any Servant in play, the attack must destroy those Servants first and only then can it reach their Seals.
karmicReflection.flavor: What you send returns, in kind.

bloodAtonement.name: Blood Atonement
bloodAtonement.effect: Costs no HP. Played ONLY when one of YOUR own Seals is about to be destroyed. To save the Seal, 2 cards leave your hand at random. You do not pick them and neither does your opponent, so it is always a gamble. If you have fewer than 2 cards in hand, this card cannot be played.

runicWarden.name: Runic Warden
runicWarden.effect: While Runic Warden is on your tableau, ALL of your Seals gain the 'untargetable' status. Runic Warden itself is always targetable. As a normal Servant, the Servant Shield rule applies: rivals must destroy Wardens (and any other Servant) before they can target Seals.

glacialAberration.name: Glacial Aberration
glacialAberration.effect: When this Servant is destroyed, whoever destroyed it skips their entire next turn (Focus, Action, and Closing); they may still play Interventions during that skipped turn. The penalty applies even when the destruction is indirect, such as a Shadow Slayer's entry or a Karmic Reflection redirect. Only destruction triggers it: having this Servant stolen, for example by Mind Parasite, does not.
glacialAberration.flavor: A winter that keeps its own time.

shadowSlayer.name: Shadow Slayer
shadowSlayer.effect: When you place Shadow Slayer, if any rival has at least one Servant you MUST choose one of those Servants and destroy it immediately. If a Glacial Aberration is destroyed this way, YOU receive its skip-next-turn penalty. If no rival Servant exists, the entry effect fizzles but Shadow Slayer still enters play.

rulesDoc.title: Vaerum: Heirs of the Ether
rulesDoc.subtitle: The Complete Rulebook
rulesDoc.tldr: 2 to 4 players. 30 to 60 minutes. Ages 14+. New here? Open the Guide from the menu and it walks the whole table through a game, step by step.
rulesDoc.intro: At the centre of all things lies the Ether, a sourceless force, fractured long ago. Its shards have changed hands through silent treaties, whispered threats, and quiet betrayals. You sit at the table now: a visionary, a schemer, one of the few who can still bend the Ether to a will. There is no friendship here. There is no mercy. There is no luck. Only manipulation, strategy, and the unblinking war for power.

sections.title: 1. Components
body: Vaerum plays with a single deck of 72 cards. Nothing else is required.
Distribution:
Seal (Violet) x16: 4 unique seals, 4 copies each.
Spell (Crimson) x24: 5 unique spells in counts of 2 to 8.
Intervention (Azure) x16: 3 unique cards in counts of 4 to 8.
Servant (Verdant) x16: 3 unique cards in counts of 4 to 8.
Colour key:
SEAL grants passive power and forms the path to victory.
SPELL is a single-use attack; resolved, then discarded.
INTERVENTION costs no HP and may be played at any time.
SERVANT shields your Seals and offers unique abilities.

sections.title: 2. Setup
body: Shuffle the 72-card deck thoroughly.
Deal 5 cards to each player; keep them hidden.
Place the remaining deck face-down at the centre.

Each player reveals one card from the deck; the highest category in the order Servant, Spell, Seal, Intervention chooses the first player. Re-draw on ties. Shuffle the revealed cards back into the deck before play begins.
Play proceeds clockwise, starting with the chosen first player.
Player counts: 2 means fast and direct; 3 means balanced and tactical; 4 means the recommended chaotic, diplomatic seat.

sections.title: 3. Zones
body: Each player owns three personal zones; two are shared.
HAND: hidden cards. Rivals see the count, not the contents. Hand limit is 7; excess is discarded at Closing. Seals and Servants do nothing while in the hand.

TABLEAU: face-up zone holding your Seals and Servants. Maximum 4 Seals and 3 Servants (the two limits are separate). When you CREATE a Seal or Servant from your hand and that row is already full, you may first Discard one of your own of the SAME type to make room. That is your choice and part of the same Create, at no extra HP. A placed card is committed: it leaves your tableau only by being destroyed, sacrificed (Ether Resonance), discarded to make room, or stolen, and never returns to your hand.
DECK (shared): face-down central pile. When it empties, Ether Resonance is triggered (see section 9).
DISCARD (shared): face-up pile of spent Spells, cancelled cards, destroyed Seals and Servants.

sections.title: 4. Turn flow
body: On your turn, perform the three phases below in this exact order. Phases cannot be skipped or reordered.

PHASE 1, FOCUS: Draw 2 cards from the deck. If it empties mid-draw, Ether Resonance resolves first and the remaining draws come from the new deck. With Time Rift in play, draw 3 (4, 5, 6 with additional copies).
PHASE 2, ACTION: You have 2 HP this turn (3 to 5 if Crimson Monolith is in play, capped at 5). Spend each HP on any single action below, in any combination. Unspent HP is lost; it does not roll over.
CREATE (1 HP): cast 1 Spell from hand, or place 1 Seal or 1 Servant onto your tableau.
STUDY (1 HP): draw 1 extra card from the deck.

CLEANSE (1 HP): discard 1 card from your hand, then draw 1 card. The card drawn by Cleanse cannot be discarded by another Cleanse in the same turn. Cleanse keeps your hand the same size (a swap), while Study grows it by one; reach for Cleanse when your hand is full or to cycle out a card you cannot use, and for Study when you simply want more cards.

PHASE 3, CLOSING: First, every active Seal trigger fires (Necromancer's Eye takes its card from the Discard now). Then check the hand limit and discard down to 7 if needed. A card just taken by Necromancer's Eye counts toward the limit, and it can be the one you discard. After Closing is complete, if you have at least 3 Seals on your tableau you MAY declare Ascension (see section 10).

sections.title: 5. Card-type rules
body: SEALS are permanent. They do nothing while in your hand. Their effects activate only on your tableau.
SPELLS cost 1 HP. You must declare a legal target before casting. If every potential target is untargetable, the spell cannot be cast and HP is not spent.
INTERVENTIONS cost no HP. They may be played at any moment, on anyone's turn, as a reaction to the most recent action.
Karmic Reflection and Blood Atonement are SELFISH: usable only to protect YOURSELF or your own cards.

Silence! is UNIVERSAL: usable to cancel any newly cast Spell or Intervention, regardless of target. Placement (Create) of a Seal or Servant is NOT a Spell or Intervention, so Silence! cannot stop a tableau placement.

SERVANTS: the SERVANT SHIELD is absolute. While a player has at least one Servant on their tableau, no rival may target any of that player's Seals directly. Even targetable cards like Runic Warden cannot be skipped; the rival must clear every Servant first.

sections.title: 6. Chained reactions
body: When an action is taken, any player may add an Intervention to the chain in response. The chain stays open until everyone passes; it then resolves from top to bottom, the most recent card resolves first.

Example: A casts Ether Strike (bottom). B answers with Silence! (middle). A answers with another Silence! (top). Resolution: A's Silence! cancels B's Silence!; both go to Discard. The bottom Ether Strike resolves normally.

Silence! interactions: a cancelled card and the Silence! that cancelled it both go to the Discard. The HP of a cancelled spell is NOT refunded. Karmic Reflection can be cancelled by Silence!; in that case the reflection never happens and the original attack hits its target.

sections.title: 7. Untargetable status
body: Some effects make their subject immune to enemy actions. Untargetability is layered: each independent layer must be removed individually.
Veil of Void: your other Seals and your hand are untargetable by enemy spells and effects. Veil of Void itself is always targetable.

Runic Warden: while on your tableau, all of your Seals are untargetable. Runic Warden is a Servant and obeys the Servant Shield rule; rivals must still destroy all your Servants before targeting your Seals.

Stacking: if both Veil of Void and Runic Warden are in play, your Seals are protected by two separate layers. In practice the rival must clear every Servant (including Runic Warden) and then destroy Veil of Void before reaching the Seals beneath.

sections.title: 8. Seal stacking
body: Multiple copies of the same Seal can sit on your tableau. New copies activate starting from the turn after they are placed (Necromancer's Eye is the lone exception, it activates the same turn).
Time Rift: stacks. Each copy adds +1 to Focus draws. (1=3, 2=4, 3=5, 4=6.)
Crimson Monolith: stacks, capped. Each copy adds +1 HP up to a hard cap of 5 total HP.
Necromancer's Eye: stacks. Each copy takes one more card from the Discard at your Closing.
Veil of Void: does NOT stack. Extra copies provide no extra benefit.

sections.title: 9. Ether Resonance
body: When the central deck is fully exhausted, play freezes. The Discard is taken, shuffled, and becomes the new deck. This shuffle is the one and only Ether Resonance event of that cycle.
Sacrifice order: every player loses one card in the following priority:
First, the tableau: choose any one Seal or Servant on your tableau and send it to the Discard. The choice is yours.
Then, the hand: ONLY if your tableau is completely empty does the resonance reach your hand. Discard 1 random card.
If a draw effect was mid-resolution when the deck emptied, resonance resolves fully first; remaining draws come from the new deck.
If both the deck and the Discard are empty, Resonance does not trigger and no new deck is formed. Effects that require a draw simply fail to draw for this attempt; play continues.

sections.title: 10. Victory, Ascension
body: DECLARATION: at the end of your Closing phase, if you have at least 3 Seals on your tableau, you MAY declare Ascension. Declaration is irrevocable.

TRIAL: every other player gets one full turn before the turn returns to you. During the Trial, anyone may use Spells, Interventions, and Servants to destroy, steal, or disable your Seals. Interventions are unrestricted.
VICTORY: when the turn returns to you, BEFORE Focus begins, if you still have at least 3 Seals intact on your tableau, you win the game.
Only one active Ascension exists at a time. Other players must wait for it to resolve (in success or failure) before they can declare their own.
Reaching a third Seal during the Trial does NOT grant a new declaration; Ascension is declared only at the end of your own turn.
If you lose Seals during the Trial and have fewer than 3 when the turn returns, the Ascension fails and play continues normally.

sections.title: 11. Card encyclopedia
body: Every card resolves exactly as written on its face. When a card text and a general rule appear to disagree, the card text wins.
SEALS (16 total, 4 unique × 4 copies):
Time Rift: During your Focus phase you draw 3 cards instead of 2. Each additional copy adds +1 draw (max 4 copies, 6 cards). Becomes active from the turn AFTER it is placed.
Veil of Void: Your other Seals and your hand cannot be targeted by enemy spells or effects. Veil of Void itself remains targetable. Active immediately when placed. Multiple copies do not stack.
Crimson Monolith: During your Action phase you spend 3 HP instead of 2. Each additional copy adds +1 HP, but your total HP can never exceed 5. Becomes active from the turn AFTER it is placed.

Necromancer's Eye: At the start of your Closing phase, before the hand-limit check, take the top card of the Discard into your hand (each extra copy takes one more). Because it happens before the limit check, if your hand goes over 7 you discard down as usual and may discard the card you just took, so it never costs you a card you wanted. If the Discard is empty, nothing happens. Active the turn it is placed.
SPELLS (24 total):
Ether Strike: 1 HP. Destroy 1 card on a rival's tableau. Servant Shield applies.

Shadow Theft: 1 HP. Choose a rival; their hand is shuffled face-down (hidden from everyone) and 1 card is taken from it at random into your hand. Neither player chooses which. Blocked by Veil of Void.
Ancient Sight: 1 HP. Draw 3 cards from the deck. Self-targeted only; no opponent involved.

Mind Parasite: 1 HP. Move one Servant from a rival's tableau to yours; it keeps its ONGOING effects and counts toward your 3-Servant limit (a one-time on-entry effect, like Shadow Slayer's, does not re-fire on a move). It needs a free Servant slot: with 3 already it cannot be cast, because a steal is never allowed to discard your own to make room (that voluntary swap is only for a Create from your hand). Taking control is not destroying, so a stolen Glacial Aberration does not trigger its skip penalty.
Twist of Fate: 1 HP. Swap every card in your hand with every card in a chosen rival's hand. Blocked by Veil of Void.
INTERVENTIONS (16 total):
Silence!: 0 HP. Cancel a freshly cast Spell or Intervention. Both cards go to the Discard. Universal target.
Karmic Reflection: 0 HP. Selfish. Cancels an attack aimed at you or your cards in play and rebounds it onto the attacker, where the attacker's own Servant Shield applies.
Blood Atonement: 0 HP. Selfish. When one of your Seals is about to be destroyed, 2 cards leave your hand at random to save it. Nobody picks them.
SERVANTS (16 total):
Runic Warden: While in play, ALL of your Seals gain untargetable. Servant Shield still applies, so rivals must clear your Wardens first.

Glacial Aberration: When destroyed, the destroyer skips their entire next turn (Focus, Action, Closing); Interventions are still allowed during the skip. Only destruction triggers this, not being stolen (e.g. by Mind Parasite).
Shadow Slayer: On entry, if any rival has a Servant you MUST destroy one. If it kills a Glacial Aberration this way, YOU receive the skip penalty.

sections.title: 12. Edge-case guide
body: Q. What happens to a Spell cancelled by Silence!? A. The cancelled Spell and the Silence! both go to the Discard. The caster's 1 HP is not refunded.
Q. Can Time Rift placed this turn give me extra cards? A. No. Its effect starts on your next turn, Focus has already happened this turn.
Q. Two Time Rifts, how many cards? A. 4 (each copy adds +1).
Q. Crimson Monolith placed this turn, does it give me 3 HP now? A. No. It activates next turn.
Q. Necromancer's Eye placed this turn, does it fire at this Closing? A. Yes. Its trigger is during Closing, which is after Action.
Q. Must I keep the card Necromancer's Eye makes me take? A. No. You take it first, then check the hand limit, so if you are over 7 you can discard it (or any other card) that same Closing.
Q. Shadow Slayer enters but no rival has Servants? A. The entry effect fizzles. Shadow Slayer still enters as a normal Servant.
Q. Shadow Slayer kills a Glacial Aberration on entry, who skips? A. You do. The destroyer pays the penalty.

Q. When Karmic Reflection rebounds an attack, does the attacker's Servant Shield apply? A. Yes. The rebounding attack must respect the attacker's Servant Shield, so it has to clear their Servants before it can reach their Seals.
Q. Blood Atonement needs how many cards in hand? A. At least 2. With fewer, it cannot be played.
Q. Who picks the 2 cards Blood Atonement discards? A. Nobody. They leave your hand at random, so paying the cost is always a gamble.

Q. Mind Parasite when your tableau already has 3 Servants? A. It cannot be cast (no HP spent): a steal needs a free Servant slot and is never allowed to discard your own to make room. That discard-to-make-room swap is only for a Create from your hand.

Q. My Servant row is full (3) and I want to play another from my hand? A. As part of that Create, first Discard one of your own Servants to make room (your choice; no extra HP). A card already on your tableau can never be taken back into your hand; it leaves only by being destroyed, sacrificed, discarded to make room, or stolen.

Q. If I take a Glacial Aberration with Mind Parasite, do I skip a turn? A. No. Taking control is not destroying, so its skip-a-turn penalty does not trigger; it only fires when the Aberration is destroyed.
Q. Shadow Theft against a hand protected by Veil of Void? A. Not legal; cannot be cast.
Q. Twist of Fate against Veil of Void? A. Not legal; the targeted player's hand is shielded.
Q. Can a player skipped by Glacial Aberration still play Interventions? A. Yes. Interventions are reactive and ignore turn order.
Q. The deck empties mid-draw? A. Ether Resonance resolves fully first; the draw effect then completes from the new deck.

sections.title: 13. Quick reference
body: Phases: Focus, Action, Closing, (optional) Ascension declaration.
HP: 0 HP for Interventions; 1 HP per Create, Study, or Cleanse; 2 HP base; up to 5 HP with Crimson Monolith.
Limits: Hand 7, Seals 4, Servants 3, HP cap 5.
Card totals: 16 Seals + 24 Spells + 16 Interventions + 16 Servants = 72.
Closing order: Seal triggers first, then hand-limit check.

sections.title: 14. The digital table
body: Vaerum on the web is a sandbox. There is no automated rules engine; players follow this rulebook themselves.

Left-press drags a card. Right-click flips the stack under the cursor. Ctrl plus left-press drags the whole stack. Scroll flips a single card; Ctrl plus scroll flips the whole stack. Shift plus scroll rotates 90° sideways; over a pile it turns and squares up the whole stack. Press G to gather the stack, M to shuffle it, and D to tidy your own area into a grouped, deck-like layout.

Your zone is the blurred strip along the bottom of your screen. Cards there are private to you; rivals see only their backs and can infer the count. You cannot drop a card inside an opponent's zone; leave it on the rim and the owner draws it in.
When everyone leaves, the room closes. Leaving the room opens a fresh one with a new link, with you as its host.

rulesDoc.closing: Show no mercy at the table.

resetDeckConfirm.title: Reset deck?
resetDeckConfirm.body: Every card on the table is gathered, shuffled face-down and returned to the deck. This affects all players.

restartGameConfirm.title: Restart the guide?
restartGameConfirm.body: The rulebook guide goes back to its first step for everyone. The cards on the table are left exactly as they are.

guide.title: Guide
guide.minimizeTurnOnly: You can minimize the guide once the turns begin.
guide.yourTurn: It is your turn. Tap the check when you are done.
guide.introBody: This guide walks the whole table through a game, one step at a time. It only suggests what to do next and never moves your cards, so you stay free to move, flip and shuffle them however you like. Tip: to begin from a freshly shuffled deck, use Reset deck from the menu first, then press Start.
guide.introWaiting: Waiting for the host to start the game.
guide.pickWait: The host is choosing the first player.

shuffle.title: Deal
shuffle.body: Shuffle the deck and deal 5 cards to each player to keep hidden. Leave the rest face down in the centre as the draw pile. The host taps the check once everyone has their hand.

reveal.title: Who goes first
reveal.body: To decide who starts, each player draws one card from the deck and reveals it. The highest type goes first, in this order: Servant, then Spell, then Seal, then Intervention. If two players tie, only they draw again. Then shuffle those revealed cards back into the deck. The host taps the check when this is done.

chooseFirst.title: First player
chooseFirst.body: The host picks the player who revealed the highest card. That player takes the first turn, and play continues clockwise from there.

focus.title: Focus
focus.body: Draw 2 cards from the deck. Draw more if you have Time Rift seals in play. Tap the check when you have drawn.

action.title: Action
action.body: You have 2 HP to spend this turn (up to 5 with Crimson Monolith seals in play). Each HP buys one action: Create (play a Spell, Seal or Servant), Study (draw 1 card), or Cleanse (discard 1 card, then draw 1). Interventions are free and can be played any time. Tap the check when you are done.

closing.title: Closing
closing.body: Trigger your active Seal effects, then discard down to 7 cards in hand. If you have 3 or more Seals, you may declare Ascension. Tap the check to pass the turn to the next player.
